headline: Political Appointees in GLCs Earn RM680M Combined
context: A governance watchdog analysis found that 342 political appointees across 48 major GLCs and statutory bodies earned a combined RM680 million in directors' fees, salaries, and benefits in 2025, with many holding multiple concurrent board positions.

cards.big: 342 political appointees earn RM680 million from GLC boards: average RM1.99 million each for part-time oversight roles
cards.sub: 48 individuals hold 3 or more concurrent board seats, collecting RM150,000-RM400,000 per board annually

cards.big: GLC board fees grew 34% since 2022 while average worker wages in the same GLCs grew only 4.8%
cards.sub: Directors' fees at Petronas, TNB, and MAHB average RM420,000 per board seat, more than 10x the median employee salary

cards.big: 87% of political GLC appointees have no professional qualification in the industry their GLC operates in
cards.sub: Former politicians and party officials appointed to energy, aviation, and technology boards they have no expertise in

cards.big: GLC board appointment process has no public criteria, no competitive selection, and no published performance evaluation
cards.sub: Same individual can sit on 5 GLC boards simultaneously, averaging 4 hours monthly per board in actual meeting time

cards.big: Paying RM680 million to political appointees for 4 hours of monthly meetings isn't corporate governance. It's a political patronage system with a corporate wrapper.
Like paying a security guard CEO wages to stand in the lobby four hours a month. The cost is corporate, but the purpose is political.
cards.sub: Board fees for minimal oversight create a patronage pipeline disguised as corporate governance

cards.big: Should all GLC board appointments require open competitive selection with mandatory industry expertise and a 2-board maximum?
